coredata.dc:title: Application of carbon dots synthesized with amino acid as precursor in the detection of phloroglucinol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Min
Hou, Aiying
Yang, Dou
Shao, Tong
Li, Rui
Hong, Min
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (CDs) were successfully applied in the detection of phloroglucinol. CDs were hydrothermally synthesized from L-phenylalanine (L-phe) and L-leucine (L-leu) in alkaline aqueous solution. The morphology, size and functional groups of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The average size of CDs was 4.26 ± 1.77 nm. The main elements of CDs were C, N, O and functional groups were carboxyl, hydroxyl, pyridine and amino groups. CDs showed bright blue fluorescence with the quantum yield (QY) at 12.82 % (λex = 337 nm and λem = 437 nm). In the presence of phloroglucinol (PG), however, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched significantly. It could be ascribed from hydrogen bonds formed between PG and functional groups on the surface of CDs. There was a good linear response between the fluorescence quenching of CDs and the concentration of PG in the range of 0.5–150 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) at 12 nM. The strategy can be used to detect PG qualitatively and quantitatively with the advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, fast response and high specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino acid
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Phloroglucinol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A), size distribution histogram (B), HRTEM (C), FT-IR spectrum (D), XPS full-scan spectrum (E), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (F), O 1 s (G), N 1 s (H).

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of CDs (inset: photographs of CDs under daylight and UV light) (A), fluorescence spectra of different systems (B), Stern-Volmer plots of CDs + PG at different temperatures (C), fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + PG (D).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of PG with various concentrations (A), corresponding relationship between F0-F and PG concentration (B), linear response of F0-F and concentration of PG (C), photographs of CDs in the presence of PG (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200 μM) under the daylight and UV light (D), effect of organic molecules (E) and amino acids (F) on the fluorescence of CDs.
Schematic illustration for CDs synthesis and the detection of PG.
Comparison of analytical performance for PG detection using different methods.

head.ce:title: Application of carbon dots synthesized with amino acid as precursor in the detection of phloroglucinol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (CDs) were successfully applied in the detection of phloroglucinol. CDs were hydrothermally synthesized from L-phenylalanine (L-phe) and L-leucine (L-leu) in alkaline aqueous solution. The morphology, size and functional groups of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The average size of CDs was 4.26 ± 1.77 nm. The main elements of CDs were C, N, O and functional groups were carboxyl, hydroxyl, pyridine and amino groups. CDs showed bright blue fluorescence with the quantum yield (QY) at 12.82 % (λex = 337 nm and λem = 437 nm). In the presence of phloroglucinol (PG), however, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched significantly. It could be ascribed from hydrogen bonds formed between PG and functional groups on the surface of CDs. There was a good linear response between the fluorescence quenching of CDs and the concentration of PG in the range of 0.5–150 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) at 12 nM. The strategy can be used to detect PG qualitatively and quantitatively with the advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, fast response and high specificity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phloroglucinol (PG), also known as 1, 3, 5-trihydroxybenzene, is a white powdery organic substance commonly used for chemical synthesis. PG is widely found in natural plants and can also be synthesized by chemical industry [1–4]. The forms of PG in solution include enol, ketone and dienone [5]. It is very versatile and can be used as precursor materials of CDs [6–11], antibacterial agent [12], antioxidant [13–16] and affected plant rooting [17,18]. With the development of modern industry, phenolic wastewater has threatened the ecological environment and human health [19–22]. Wastewater containing PG, if discharged untreated, poses a serious threat to the environment and human health [23]. Excessive intake of PG can lead to nausea, vomiting, lack of consciousness, suffocation and even life-threatening conditions. It is reported that PG can induce type II Kounis syndrome due to its toxicity [24].

Due to the hazardous effects of PG, the detection of PG becomes our top concern in the environment, in untreated plant effluents and medical waste. Since 1963, there has been qualitative and quantitative analysis of PG [25]. In the past few decades, the detection of PG has focused on titrimetry [26], chromatography [27,28], chemiluminescence [29], and electrochemical measurements [30–32]. The titration method required a longer detection time. The detection limit of chemiluminescence method is relatively high and the electrochemical detection method faced the problems of complex synthesis steps and narrow linear range in detecting materials. Compared with these methods, fluorescence method has the advantages of simple material preparation, short detection time, wide linear range, and low detection limit. Thus, the fluorescence detection of PG can be encouraged.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of carbon based nanoparticles with diameter less than 10 nm, are popular because of the advantages of easy synthesis, easy preparation of portable optical devices, stable nature, environment friendly and low toxicity [33]. Since the discovery of CDs, they have been frequently used in various fields, such as catalysis [34–36], sensing [19–22], cell imaging [37], preparation of light emitting diode lamps (LED lamps), and anti-counterfeiting, since their discovery [38–41]. At present, CDs have been widely used for detecting environmental pollutants. Wei et al. [7] used CDs to detect the Fe3+ in lake water. CDs synthesized from Ganpu tea were successfully applied to detect p-nitrophenol (p-NP) in lake and tap water [19]. CDs combined with molecular imprinting technology have also been successfully applied to detect various nitrophenols (o-NP, m-NP, and p-NP) in environmental water samples.

In this work, CDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using L-leucine (L-leu) and L-phenylalanine (L-phe) in alkaline medium (Scheme 1 ). CDs show bright blue fluorescence with excitation and emission wavelength of 337 nm and 437 nm. In the presence of PG, the fluorescence of CDs decreases gradually with the increase of PG concentration. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was discussed. The static quenching mode was proposed. And then a turn-off fluorometric analysis of PG was constructed based on CDs. Furthermore, the present sensing method was successfully applied in the detection of PG in environmental samples.
For the detailed information of apparatus and chemicals used in the experiment, please refer to S1 and S2 (in Supporting Information).

As shown in Scheme 1, CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method in the experiment [33]. Briefly, 218 mg L-leu, 32 mg L-phe and 40 mg NaOH were added to a Teflon lined reactor (25 mL) containing 10 ml deionized water and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. After the dispersion of CDs was dialyzed in 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, it was stored at 4 °C refrigerator for subsequent experiments. The synthesis of CDs by Keerthana et al. [32] requires pre-treatment of the reactants, and the synthesized CDs also need to be modified on the electrode for subsequent detection. The tedious steps wasted detection time.

PG solid was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a series of standard solutions of PG with appropriate dilution. 5 μL of PG aqueous solution with different concentration was added to 245 μL CDs solution. After the mixture was shaken for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength at 337 nm and the fluorescence intensity at 437 nm were recorded.

To test the practical application of CDs in the detection of PG, lake water and tap water samples were employed as real samples. All water samples were collected from Liaocheng University campus. Water samples were firstly filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane, and then diluted with deionized water. Because the content of PG in water samples was too low to be determined directly by this method, the standard addition method was used. In order to verify the accuracy of the results for PG detection using CDs, the same samples were monitored by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) technology, which was used as reference method [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions and stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of reactant ratio and NaOH concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were studied (Fig. S1, in Supporting Information). The optimum condition was evaluated by the fluorescence of CDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs got to the top with the molar ratio of L-leu and L-phe and the concentration of NaOH at 5:1 and 1 mM, respectively.

Considering of the stability of CDs, the light exposure, storage temperature, pH and photostablity of CDs were tested. All the data were evaluated by the calculation of F/F0, where F0 and F was the fluorescence intensity of fresh CDs and CDs after exposure, storage or illumination, respectively (Fig. S2, in Supporting Information). The F/F0 of CDs decreased to below 90 % after being placed in daylight for 40 h (Fig. S2A, in Supporting Information). When CDs products were stored at 4 °C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs could keep about 90 % after 4 days (inset of Fig. S2B, in Supporting Information). In addition, the alkaline medium was more useful for the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S2C, in Supporting Information). Furthermore, photostability of CDs was also investigated using the relationship of F/F0 and illumination time with a 300 W Xe lamp (Fig. S2D, in Supporting Information). Besides CDs, the photostability of 2′, 7′-dichromofluorescein, quinine sulfonate (QS) and black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) were also studied as reference. It was found that after illumination for 120 min, the F/F0 value of 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein, QS, CDs, BPQDs was 9.45 %, 14.16 %, 75.31 % (CDs) and 70.35 %, respectively. The corresponding photographs for four species were illustrated in Fig. S3 (in Supporting Information). It was clearly observed that only CDs displayed bright blue fluorescence after illumination of Xe lamp for 120 min. It was deduced that the photostability of CDs was better than that of 2′, 7-Dichlorofluorescein, QS and BPQDs.

The morphology, size and functional groups of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). It was illustrated that CDs were almost spherical and monodispersed particles without obvious aggregation (Fig. 1 A). The corresponding particle size of CDs was 4.26 ± 1.77 nm through statistic of 100 particles (Fig. 1B). HRTEM images shown in Fig. 1C illustrated the lattice spacing of CDs at 0.202 nm, corresponding to the lattice stripes in the (100) plane of graphene [42,43]. The functional groups of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 1D). The absorption peaks of 2958.06 cm−1, 1582.52 cm−1 were attributed to C-H and C = O stretching vibrations, respectively [44]. The peaks of 1408.87 cm−1 and 1509.72 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C = N and C-N. The absorption band in the range of 950–650 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibration of C-H on the benzene ring.

The elemental composition of CDs was analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). According to Fig. 1E, except the Na peak observed in NaOH added as an alkaline environment, the main constituent elements of CDs were C, N, and O. The atomic percentage of C, N, and O was 61.61 %, 8.67 % and 25.75 %, respectively. There were five peaks of HRXPS for C 1 s (Fig. 1F) including C-C/C = C at 284.4 e V [45], C-N for 285.35 eV [46], C-O (286.4 eV) [47], C = N (287.7 eV) [42] and C = O at 288.2 eV [47], respectively. For the HRXPS spectrum of O 1 s the peaks at 530.75 eV and 531.3 eV were attributed to C = O-OH [48] and C = O [49] (Fig. 1G). The peaks for HRXPS of N 1s were pyridine N at 399 eV [48] and N-H at 400.75 eV [50] (Fig. 1H).

The optical properties of CDs were characterized using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The dispersion of CDs was colorless under a fluorescent lamp and exhibited bright blue fluorescence in UV light. The fluorescence characteristics of CDs were characterized using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. There was no obvious peak in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2 A). The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of CDs was at 337 nm and 437 nm, respectively (Fig. 2A). As the excitation wavelength gradually increased, the emission peak of CDs shifted red, and the fluorescence peak intensity went up and down leading to the maximum excitation at 337 nm (Fig. 2B). The quantum yield (QY) of CDs were measured using the fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000). The QY of CDs was tested at 12.82 % (Fig. S4, in Supporting Information).

CDs prepared in alkaline medium displayed bright blue fluorescence, while PG could quench the fluorescence of CDs. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of PG was discussed. In the dispersion of CDs, there existed enol and keto isomerism of PG under alkaline condition leading to the formation of dienone or pyroquinone [5,32,51]. There were hydrogen bonds between ketone isomerization and the amino or amide groups on the surface of CDs. There was electron transfer through the hydrogen bonds resulting in fluorescence quench of CDs.

For the fluorescence quenching, there were two ways including the dynamic and static modes. The dynamic quenching was possibly ascribed to the collision between the excited fluorophore (CDs) and quencher (PG). The collision frequency increased with temperature, which leaded to the increase of dynamic quenching constant. On the other side, the static one was mainly caused by the formation of a complex between the ground state of fluorophore and the quenching agent. The stability of the composite decreased with increasing temperature, so the quenching constant decreased instead with increasing temperature. F0, F, KSV, [Q] represented the fluorescence intensity of CDs without and with PG, the quenching constant and the concentration of PG, respectively. Fig. 2C illustrated the Stern-Volmer plots for the quenching of PG on the fluorescence of CDs at 4 °C, 22 °C and 41 °C. By Stern-Volmer equation (eq. (1).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q

The fluorescence quenching constant (K SV) was calculated from Stern-Volmer plots as 1.2 × 104 M−1, 8.5 × 103 M−1 and 5.4 × 103 M−1 at 4 °C, 22 °C and 41 °C, respectively. It was found that the value of K SV decreased with temperature increase. This was the clue of static quenching mode. For the further evidence, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs with or without PG was measured at 3.33 ns and 3.19 ns (Fig. 2D), respectively. It indicated that the formation of complex between PG and CDs leaded to the fluorescence quenching.

The sensitivity of PG detection was carried out under the optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 437 nm was gradually quenched by PG with increasing concentration from 0 to 200 μM. The corresponding peak intensity was almost in a line with the concentration of PG (Fig. 3B). There was a good linear relationship between the quenching intensity of CDs (F 0-F) and the concentration of PG (0.5–150 μM) (R2 = 0.9984) (Fig. 3C). The linear equation is F 0-F = 31.3518[PG] + 754.5376. According to the IUPAC recommendation, the limit of detection (LOD) of PG could be calculated at 12 nM using the equation of 3.29 S B/m where S B was the blank standard deviation (n = 10) and m was the slope of the standard curve. By comparison of the results for the determination of PG, it was found that there was lower LOD and wider linear range for the proposed approach (Table 1 ). It was also observed that the fluorescence of CDs was weaker with higher concentration of PG though there was no color change of CDs in daylight (Fig. 3D). The concentration of PG was measured using a titration method by Amin et al [26]. This method was based on complex reactions between phenolic substances and excess bromine, and it had low sensitivity. Meanwhile the electrochemical methods had narrow linear range and high detection limits. Relatively, the fluorescence method in this work has short detection time, wide linear range, and lower detection limit.

In order to study the specificity of CDs in detecting PG, small organic molecules and amino acids were selected as interference substances in detecting PG. Small organic molecules include RC, MPD, 4-ASA, 2-nitrophenol, dipicolinic acid, mandelic acid, terephthalic acid (PTA), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2, 4-DNT) and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (M−APBA). Amino acids are L-glutamic acid (Glu), L-phenylalanine (Phe), L-proline (Pro), L-cysteine (Cys), L-histidine (His), L-threonine (Thr) and L-glycine (Gly), L-Serine (Ser), L-threonine (Thr) and L-leucine (Leu). It can be observed that only PG can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs while there is almost no change of fluorescence for other substances with concentration of 300 μM (Fig. 3E and F).

To investigate whether this method can be applied in practice, PG was measured in lake water and tap water samples. The lake water and tap water samples were pre-treated according to the experimental section. The results are listed in Table S1 (in Supporting Information), with a recovery rate of 97.2 %-104.5 % and a relative standard deviation of 0.69 %-1.99 %. Meanwhile, HPLC was used as the reference method to detect the same sample. It can be deduced that the results obtained by the two methods are consistent with each other, indicating that the present method can be used to detect PG in real samples.

In summary, CDs were synthesized with blue fluorescence using two amino acids as precursor materials and successfully used to detect PG in lake and tap water samples. CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, fluorescence and absorption spectral apparatus. The bright blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched by PG significantly due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the tautomerism of PG and CDs. In this case, a reliable turn-off fluorescence sensing method for PG detection was established. The linear range for the concentration of PG is 0.5–150 μM with a LOD of 12 nM. By comparison with other methods the proposed one has wider linear concentration range and lower LOD. The detection of PG is specific and almost unaffected by most organic compounds and amino acids. Therefore, CDs have good application prospects in the field of detecting PG in environmental samples.

Min Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Aiying Hou: Visualization. Dou Yang: Visualization. Tong Shao: Visualization, Conceptualization. Rui Li: Visualization. Min Hong: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Qiaoli Yue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Natural Science Foundation (ZR2022MB052) and Graduate Education Quality Improvement Plan of Shandong Province (SDYJG21198), and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022 and 318012116).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
